problems.problem: Explain why DNA replication is described as 'semi-conservative.' Include reference to the role of DNA polymerase in your answer.

step_by_step.explanation: DNA replication is described as semi-conservative because each newly synthesized DNA molecule consists of one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesized (daughter) strand. This was demonstrated conclusively by the Meselson-Stahl experiment in 1958 using isotopes of nitrogen (¹⁵N and ¹⁴N). In this model, the two strands of the original DNA double helix separate, and each serves as a template for the synthesis of a complementary new strand. After replication, each daughter DNA molecule conserves one of the original parent strands—hence 'semi' (half) 'conservative' (preserved). This contrasts with the conservative model (where the original molecule remains intact and an entirely new copy is made) and the dispersive model (where parental strands are fragmented and interspersed with new DNA).

DNA polymerase is the key enzyme that carries out semi-conservative replication. Its specific roles include: (1) Template-directed synthesis: DNA polymerase reads the exposed parental strand (template) and adds complementary nucleotides to the growing new strand according to base-pairing rules (A with T, G with C). This ensures that the new strand is an exact complement of the template, preserving the genetic sequence. (2) Directionality: DNA polymerase synthesizes DNA only in the 5'→3' direction, meaning it adds nucleotides to the 3' hydroxyl group of the growing strand. This requires the enzyme to move along the template strand in the 3'→5' direction. (3) Leading and lagging strands: Because DNA polymerase can only synthesize in one direction, replication occurs continuously on one strand (the leading strand) and discontinuously on the other (the lagging strand), producing Okazaki fragments that are later joined by DNA ligase. (4) Proofreading: Many DNA polymerases have 3'→5' exonuclease activity, allowing them to detect and remove mismatched nucleotides immediately after incorporation. This proofreading function dramatically reduces the error rate to approximately one mistake per billion nucleotides, ensuring faithful transmission of genetic information.

problems.final_answer: DNA replication is semi-conservative because each new DNA molecule contains one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesized (daughter) strand. DNA polymerase adds complementary nucleotides to each template strand, ensuring accurate copying of genetic information.

problems.problem: (a) State Mendel's First Law (Law of Segregation). (2 marks) (b) Explain how Mendel's experiments with pea plants demonstrated this law. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Mendel's First Law, the Law of Segregation, states that every individual carries two copies (alleles) of each gene, and these two alleles separate (segregate) during gamete formation so that each gamete receives only one allele for each gene. This segregation occurs randomly, with each gamete having an equal probability of receiving either allele. When fertilization occurs, the offspring inherit one allele from each parent, restoring the paired condition. This law explains the reappearance of recessive traits in the F₂ generation after they seemed to disappear in the F₁ generation.

Mendel demonstrated the Law of Segregation through his monohybrid crosses with pea plants, specifically using the trait for flower color (purple vs. white). The experiment proceeded as follows: (1) Parental generation (P): Mendel crossed true-breeding purple-flowered plants (PP) with true-breeding white-flowered plants (pp). All F₁ offspring were purple-flowered (Pp), showing that purple is dominant and white is recessive. (2) Self-pollination of F₁: Mendel then allowed the F₁ hybrid plants (Pp) to self-pollinate. (3) F₂ generation results: The F₂ generation produced a phenotypic ratio of approximately 3 purple : 1 white. (4) Interpretation: The reappearance of white flowers in the F₂ generation (which had been hidden in the F₁) demonstrated that the white allele had not been lost or blended, but had been segregated into separate gametes. When F₁ plants (Pp) formed gametes, the P and p alleles separated, producing 50% P-carrying gametes and 50% p-carrying gametes. Random fertilization of these gametes gave the 1:2:1 genotypic ratio (PP:Pp:pp) and the 3:1 phenotypic ratio. Mendel further confirmed segregation by performing test crosses and observing that the recessive trait bred true when self-pollinated, providing evidence that alleles remain discrete and segregate during gamete formation.

problems.final_answer: (a) The Law of Segregation states that the two alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation, so each gamete receives only one allele. (b) Mendel crossed true-breeding purple (PP) and white (pp) pea plants; all F₁ were purple (Pp). When F₁ self-pollinated, F₂ showed a 3:1 purple:white ratio, showing the white allele had segregated and reappeared in 1/4 of offspring.

problems.problem: In pea plants, tall (T) is dominant to dwarf (t). A heterozygous tall plant is crossed with a dwarf plant. (a) Draw a Punnett square to show this cross. (2 marks) (b) State the genotypic ratio of the offspring. (2 marks) (c) State the phenotypic ratio of the offspring. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: The cross is between a heterozygous tall plant (Tt) and a dwarf plant (tt). The heterozygous parent produces two types of gametes: T and t, each with equal probability (50%). The dwarf parent, being homozygous recessive, produces only one type of gamete: t. The Punnett square is set up with the gametes of one parent across the top and the gametes of the other parent down the side, showing all possible combinations in the offspring.
step_by_step.calculation:
Parental genotypes: Tt (tall) × tt (dwarf)

Gametes from Tt parent: T, t
Gametes from tt parent: t

Punnett square:

         T      t
    +--------------
  t |    Tt     tt
  t |    Tt     tt

Offspring genotypes: Tt, Tt, tt, tt

step_by_step.explanation: From the Punnett square, out of four possible offspring, two have the genotype Tt (heterozygous tall) and two have the genotype tt (homozygous recessive dwarf). There are no TT offspring because the dwarf parent cannot contribute a T allele. The genotypic ratio is therefore 2 Tt : 2 tt, which simplifies to 1 Tt : 1 tt.
step_by_step.calculation: Genotypic ratio: 2 Tt : 2 tt = 1 : 1

step_by_step.explanation: Tall (T) is dominant to dwarf (t). Therefore, individuals with at least one T allele (genotype Tt) will be tall. Individuals with the genotype tt will be dwarf. Since there are two Tt (tall) and two tt (dwarf) offspring, the phenotypic ratio is 2 tall : 2 dwarf, which simplifies to 1 tall : 1 dwarf. This 1:1 phenotypic ratio is characteristic of a test cross between a heterozygote and a homozygous recessive individual.
step_by_step.calculation: Phenotypic ratio: 2 tall : 2 dwarf = 1 : 1

problems.final_answer: (a) Punnett square: Tt parent (gametes T and t) × tt parent (gametes t) gives offspring: Tt, Tt, tt, tt. (b) Genotypic ratio: 1 Tt : 1 tt. (c) Phenotypic ratio: 1 tall : 1 dwarf.

problems.problem: In guinea pigs, black fur (B) is dominant to white fur (b), and short hair (S) is dominant to long hair (s). A guinea pig homozygous dominant for both traits is crossed with a guinea pig homozygous recessive for both traits. (a) State the genotype of each parent. (2 marks) (b) State the genotype and phenotype of all F₁ offspring. (2 marks) (c) If two F₁ offspring are crossed, what is the probability of an F₂ offspring being black with long hair? Show your working. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: The first parent is homozygous dominant for both traits. This means it carries two copies of the dominant allele for fur color (B) and two copies of the dominant allele for hair length (S). Its genotype is BBSS. The second parent is homozygous recessive for both traits, meaning it carries two copies of the recessive allele for fur color (b) and two copies of the recessive allele for hair length (s). Its genotype is bbss.
step_by_step.calculation:
Parent 1 (homozygous dominant): BBSS
Parent 2 (homozygous recessive): bbss

step_by_step.step: F₁ offspring genotype and phenotype (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: When crossing BBSS × bbss, each parent contributes one allele for each gene. The BBSS parent can only produce gametes containing B and S. The bbss parent can only produce gametes containing b and s. All F₁ offspring receive B from the first parent and b from the second (giving Bb), and S from the first parent and s from the second (giving Ss). Therefore, all F₁ offspring have the genotype BbSs. Since black (B) is dominant to white (b) and short hair (S) is dominant to long hair (s), all F₁ offspring will have black fur and short hair.
step_by_step.calculation:
F₁ genotype: BbSs (all offspring)
F₁ phenotype: black fur, short hair (all offspring)

step_by_step.step: Probability of black with long hair in F₂ (part c)
step_by_step.explanation: To find the probability of an F₂ offspring being black with long hair, we cross two F₁ individuals: BbSs × BbSs. This is a dihybrid cross. The phenotypic ratio for a dihybrid cross between two heterozygotes (when both genes assort independently) is 9:3:3:1, where 9 are dominant for both traits, 3 are dominant for the first and recessive for the second, 3 are recessive for the first and dominant for the second, and 1 is recessive for both. Black with long hair means black fur (dominant) and long hair (recessive). This corresponds to the second category in the 9:3:3:1 ratio: individuals with at least one B (for black) and homozygous ss (for long hair). The probability of this combination is 3/16. Alternatively, we can calculate using the product rule: probability of black fur (B_) is 3/4 (from Bb × Bb), and probability of long hair (ss) is 1/4 (from Ss × Ss). Multiplying these independent probabilities gives (3/4) × (1/4) = 3/16.
step_by_step.calculation:
Black with long hair = B_ ss
P(B_) from Bb × Bb = 3/4
P(ss) from Ss × Ss = 1/4
P(B_ ss) = (3/4) × (1/4) = 3/16

problems.final_answer: (a) Parent genotypes: BBSS and bbss. (b) F₁ genotype: BbSs; F₁ phenotype: black fur, short hair. (c) Probability of black with long hair in F₂: 3/16 (or 18.75%).

problems.problem: In snapdragons, red flower colour (R) is incompletely dominant to white (r). Heterozygous plants have pink flowers. (a) State the phenotype ratio from a cross between two pink snapdragons. (2 marks) (b) Explain why this is not an example of blending inheritance. (2 marks)

step_by_step.step: Phenotype ratio from cross between pink snapdragons (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: Pink snapdragons have the genotype Rr (heterozygous). A cross between two pink snapdragons is Rr × Rr. The Punnett square shows the following offspring genotypes: 1 RR (red), 2 Rr (pink), and 1 rr (white). Under incomplete dominance, the heterozygous genotype produces an intermediate phenotype (pink) rather than the dominant phenotype. Therefore, the phenotypic ratio is 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white.
step_by_step.calculation:
Rr × Rr → 1 RR : 2 Rr : 1 rr
Phenotypes: 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white

step_by_step.explanation: This is not an example of blending inheritance because the parental traits (red and white) are not permanently mixed or lost. In blending inheritance, the offspring would have an intermediate phenotype, and the original traits would be irreversibly diluted and unable to reappear in later generations. However, in incomplete dominance, the alleles remain discrete and unchanged. When two pink (Rr) snapdragons are crossed, the white and red phenotypes reappear in the offspring (1:2:1 ratio). This demonstrates that the red and white alleles have not blended into a permanent 'pink' form; they have segregated and recombined in subsequent generations. The underlying genetic material is particulate, not fluid, and Mendel's laws still apply—incomplete dominance affects the expression of the phenotype, not the segregation of alleles.
step_by_step.calculation: Reappearance of red and white phenotypes in F₂ proves alleles remain distinct and are not blended.

problems.final_answer: (a) Phenotype ratio: 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white. (b) This is not blending inheritance because the red and white alleles remain discrete and reappear in later generations (1:2:1 ratio), showing they are not permanently mixed.

problems.problem: Human blood groups are determined by three alleles: Iᴬ, Iᴮ, and Iᴼ. (a) Explain what is meant by 'codominance.' (2 marks) (b) Complete the table showing possible genotypes for each blood group. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Codominance occurs when both alleles in a heterozygote are fully expressed in the phenotype, with neither allele being dominant or recessive to the other. Unlike incomplete dominance (where the heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype), codominance results in both parental traits being simultaneously and distinctly visible. In the ABO blood group system, the Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles are codominant. When a person inherits both Iᴬ and Iᴮ (genotype IᴬIᴮ), their red blood cells produce both A antigens and B antigens, resulting in type AB blood. Both antigens are present in equal amounts, and neither overrides the other. The Iᴼ allele is recessive to both Iᴬ and Iᴮ, meaning it produces no antigen and is only expressed when homozygous (IᴼIᴼ, type O blood).

The ABO blood group system is determined by three alleles: Iᴬ (produces A antigen), Iᴮ (produces B antigen), and Iᴼ (produces no antigen). The possible genotypes for each blood group are: Blood group A: individuals with A antigens on their red blood cells can have either two copies of Iᴬ (IᴬIᴬ) or one copy of Iᴬ and one copy of Iᴼ (IᴬIᴼ), since Iᴼ is recessive and does not produce an antigen that interferes. Blood group B: similarly, individuals can be IᴮIᴮ or IᴮIᴼ. Blood group AB: individuals must have one Iᴬ and one Iᴮ (IᴬIᴮ); this is the only genotype that produces both A and B antigens due to codominance. Blood group O: individuals must be homozygous recessive (IᴼIᴼ), producing no A or B antigens.
step_by_step.calculation:
Blood group A: IᴬIᴬ, IᴬIᴼ
Blood group B: IᴮIᴮ, IᴮIᴼ
Blood group AB: IᴬIᴮ
Blood group O: IᴼIᴼ

problems.final_answer: (a) Codominance occurs when both alleles in a heterozygote are fully expressed, producing a phenotype that shows both traits simultaneously (e.g., type AB blood has both A and B antigens). (b) Table: A: IᴬIᴬ, IᴬIᴼ; B: IᴮIᴮ, IᴮIᴼ; AB: IᴬIᴮ; O: IᴼIᴼ.

problems.problem: (a) Explain how sex is determined in humans. (3 marks) (b) State the probability of a couple having a male child. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: In humans, sex is determined by the sex chromosomes: females have two X chromosomes (XX), and males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). The sex of an offspring is determined at fertilization by which type of sperm fertilizes the egg. Females produce only one type of gamete with respect to sex chromosomes: all eggs carry an X chromosome. Males produce two types of sperm: approximately half carry an X chromosome and half carry a Y chromosome. If an X-carrying sperm fertilizes the egg, the zygote will be XX (female). If a Y-carrying sperm fertilizes the egg, the zygote will be XY (male). The Y chromosome carries the SRY gene (Sex-determining Region Y) on its short arm, which triggers the development of testes and male sexual characteristics. In the absence of a Y chromosome (and therefore the SRY gene), the default developmental pathway leads to female development. Thus, the male determines the sex of the offspring because he contributes either an X or a Y chromosome.
step_by_step.calculation:
Egg: always X
Sperm: 50% X, 50% Y
Fertilization: X + X = XX (female); X + Y = XY (male)

step_by_step.explanation: Since sperm production results in approximately equal numbers of X-bearing and Y-bearing sperm, and each has an equal chance of fertilizing the egg, the probability of conceiving a male child (XY) is 1/2 or 50%. This assumes no selective factors affecting sperm viability or fertilization success. In large populations, the secondary sex ratio at birth is slightly skewed (approximately 105 males per 100 females), but the underlying probability is 0.5.

problems.final_answer: (a) Females have XX chromosomes, males have XY. Eggs carry X; sperm carry either X or Y. If an X sperm fertilizes the egg, offspring is female (XX); if Y sperm fertilizes, offspring is male (XY). (b) Probability of a male child is 1/2 (50%).

problems.problem: Haemophilia is a sex-linked recessive disorder. A carrier female has a child with a normal male. (a) State the genotypes of the parents. (2 marks) (b) Draw a genetic diagram to show the possible genotypes and phenotypes of their children. (3 marks) (c) State the probability of a son having haemophilia. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Haemophilia is an X-linked recessive disorder. The gene is located on the X chromosome, with no corresponding allele on the Y chromosome. Let Xᴴ represent the normal allele (producing clotting factors) and Xʰ represent the haemophilia allele (non-functional clotting factor). A carrier female has one normal and one affected X chromosome: her genotype is XᴴXʰ. She does not have haemophilia because the normal allele is dominant. A normal male has a normal X chromosome and a Y chromosome: his genotype is XᴴY. He does not have haemophilia and cannot pass an affected X to his children because his only X is normal.
step_by_step.calculation:
Mother (carrier female): XᴴXʰ
Father (normal male): XᴴY

step_by_step.explanation: To determine the possible genotypes and phenotypes of their children, we construct a Punnett square with the mother's gametes (Xᴴ and Xʰ) and the father's gametes (Xᴴ and Y). The father produces two types of gametes: Xᴴ and Y, each with 50% probability. The mother produces Xᴴ and Xʰ, each with 50% probability. The resulting offspring genotypes and their interpretations are: XᴴXᴴ (normal female, no carrier status, not affected), XᴴXʰ (carrier female, normal phenotype), XᴴY (normal male), XʰY (male with haemophilia).
step_by_step.calculation:
Punnett square:

         Xᴴ        Xʰ
    +-------------------
Xᴴ  |  XᴴXᴴ      XᴴXʰ
Y   |  XᴴY       XʰY

Offspring:
XᴴXᴴ: normal female
XᴴXʰ: carrier female (normal phenotype)
XᴴY: normal male
XʰY: male with haemophilia

Phenotypes: 1 normal female : 1 carrier female : 1 normal male : 1 haemophiliac male

step_by_step.step: Probability of a son having haemophilia (part c)
step_by_step.explanation: The probability of a son having haemophilia is the probability that a male child inherits the Xʰ allele from his mother and the Y chromosome from his father. From the Punnett square, among the four equally likely outcomes, one is XʰY (son with haemophilia). Alternatively, we can calculate: the probability of having a son is 1/2, and given the child is a son, the probability he inherits the mother's Xʰ allele is 1/2. The product is 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4. However, the question asks specifically for the probability that a son (not any child) has haemophilia. From the Punnett square, there are two possible male outcomes: XᴴY (normal) and XʰY (haemophiliac), each with equal probability. Therefore, among sons, the probability of having haemophilia is 1/2. If the question intended the probability among all children, it would be 1/4. The wording 'probability of a son having haemophilia' is ambiguous; the most common interpretation is: given that the child is a son, what is the chance he has haemophilia? Answer: 1/2.
step_by_step.calculation:
Among sons: 1/2 have haemophilia (XʰY) and 1/2 are normal (XᴴY).
P(son has haemophilia) = 1/2

problems.final_answer: (a) Mother: XᴴXʰ (carrier), Father: XᴴY (normal). (b) Genetic diagram shows: daughters: XᴴXᴴ (normal) and XᴴXʰ (carrier); sons: XᴴY (normal) and XʰY (haemophiliac). (c) Probability of a son having haemophilia: 1/2 (50%).

problems.problem: Describe the steps involved in constructing a genetic diagram to solve a genetics problem.

step_by_step.explanation: Begin by identifying the traits and alleles involved. Determine which allele is dominant and which is recessive (unless codominance or incomplete dominance applies). Assign clear symbols for each allele: typically, a capital letter for the dominant allele and the same letter in lowercase for the recessive allele (e.g., T for tall, t for dwarf). For sex-linked traits, indicate that the gene is on the X chromosome (e.g., Xᴴ, Xʰ). For multiple alleles, use appropriate notation (e.g., Iᴬ, Iᴮ, Iᴼ). State the genotypes of the parents based on the information given in the problem.

Determine all possible gamete types each parent can produce. According to Mendel's Law of Segregation, the two alleles for each gene separate during gamete formation. For a single gene, a heterozygote (Tt) produces two types of gametes (T and t) in equal proportions. For two independently assorting genes, a dihybrid (BbSs) produces four types of gametes (BS, Bs, bS, bs) in equal proportions (1:1:1:1). For sex-linked crosses, remember that males produce two types of gametes (X and Y) while females produce only X-bearing gametes.

Draw a Punnett square grid. Place the gametes of one parent across the top (columns) and the gametes of the other parent down the left side (rows). The number of columns equals the number of distinct gamete types from the first parent, and the number of rows equals the number of distinct gamete types from the second parent. For a monohybrid cross, this is typically a 2×2 grid; for a dihybrid cross, it is a 4×4 grid. Fill each cell by combining the column and row gametes to produce the genotype of the offspring. For each cell, write the alleles from both gametes in a consistent order (e.g., dominant allele first, then recessive, or alphabetically).

step_by_step.step: Step 4: Determine offspring genotypes and phenotypes
step_by_step.explanation: For each cell in the Punnett square, identify the genotype and determine the corresponding phenotype based on the inheritance pattern (complete dominance, incomplete dominance, codominance, sex-linkage). Count the number of times each genotype or phenotype appears. For large crosses (e.g., dihybrid), counting the 4×4 grid gives 16 cells, which can be grouped into phenotypic categories (e.g., 9:3:3:1 ratio for a standard dihybrid cross).

Express the results as genotypic ratios (e.g., 1:2:1) and/or phenotypic ratios (e.g., 3:1). For probabilities, convert ratios to fractions. For example, if 3 out of 16 cells show a particular phenotype, the probability is 3/16. State the final answer clearly, often including both the ratio and the interpretation. For problems with specific questions (e.g., 'what is the chance of a child being affected?'), express the answer as a fraction, percentage, or ratio as required.

Adjust the approach for special inheritance patterns: For sex-linked traits, include the sex chromosomes in the Punnett square and note that males inherit their X chromosome from their mother and Y from their father. For codominance, list both alleles in the phenotype (e.g., AB blood type). For incomplete dominance, the heterozygote has a distinct intermediate phenotype. For linked genes (not covered in Grade 10 typically), a different approach using recombination frequencies is required. Always verify that the diagram accounts for all genetic information provided in the problem.

problems.final_answer: Steps: (1) Identify alleles and parental genotypes. (2) Determine possible gametes for each parent. (3) Construct a Punnett square with gametes on axes. (4) Fill in offspring genotypes. (5) Determine phenotypes and count frequencies. (6) Express genotypic and phenotypic ratios or probabilities.

problems.problem: (a) Define what is meant by a 'gene mutation.' (2 marks) (b) Describe how a point mutation can affect the protein produced. (2 marks) (c) State one factor that can increase the rate of mutation. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: A gene mutation is a permanent, heritable change in the nucleotide sequence of DNA that makes up a gene. Mutations can occur spontaneously during DNA replication (due to replication errors) or be induced by external factors (mutagens). They range in scale from single nucleotide changes (point mutations) to larger insertions, deletions, or rearrangements of DNA segments. Gene mutations are the ultimate source of genetic variation, but they can also cause genetic disorders, contribute to cancer, or have neutral effects. Mutations that occur in germ cells (sperm or eggs) can be passed to offspring; mutations in somatic cells are not inherited but may contribute to cancer or aging.

step_by_step.step: Effect of point mutation on protein (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: A point mutation (substitution of a single nucleotide) can affect the resulting protein in several ways depending on the nature of the change: (1) Silent mutation: If the changed codon still codes for the same amino acid (due to the degeneracy of the genetic code), there is no change in the protein sequence. (2) Missense mutation: The changed codon codes for a different amino acid. This can alter the protein's structure and function. The effect ranges from negligible to severe—for example, the sickle cell mutation is a missense mutation (GAG to GTG) that substitutes valine for glutamic acid in hemoglobin, causing red blood cells to deform under low oxygen. (3) Nonsense mutation: The changed codon becomes a stop codon (UAA, UAG, UGA), resulting in premature termination of translation. This typically produces a truncated, non-functional protein. (4) Frameshift mutation: If the point mutation involves an insertion or deletion (indel) that is not a multiple of three nucleotides, it shifts the reading frame, altering all subsequent amino acids and often leading to a non-functional protein.
step_by_step.calculation: Example: Sickle cell mutation: GAG (glutamic acid) → GTG (valine) in β-globin gene

step_by_step.step: Factor that increases mutation rate (part c)
step_by_step.explanation: Factors that increase mutation rate are called mutagens. These include: (1) Physical mutagens: Ionizing radiation (X-rays, gamma rays) causes DNA double-strand breaks; ultraviolet (UV) radiation causes thymine dimers (covalent bonds between adjacent thymine bases). (2) Chemical mutagens: Base analogs (e.g., 5-bromouracil) incorporate into DNA and mispair; alkylating agents (e.g., ethyl methanesulfonate) add alkyl groups to bases, causing mispairing; intercalating agents (e.g., ethidium bromide, acridine dyes) insert between bases, causing frameshift mutations. (3) Biological mutagens: Certain viruses (e.g., human papillomavirus) can integrate into the host genome and disrupt genes; transposable elements ('jumping genes') can insert into genes and disrupt function. (4) Endogenous factors: Spontaneous deamination, oxidation, and replication errors also contribute to mutation, though these are not external factors.
step_by_step.calculation: Examples: UV radiation, X-rays, certain chemicals (e.g., benzene, aflatoxins), or tobacco smoke

problems.final_answer: (a) A gene mutation is a permanent change in the DNA sequence of a gene that can be passed to daughter cells. (b) A point mutation can change a single amino acid (missense), create a premature stop codon (nonsense), or have no effect (silent), altering protein structure and function. (c) Mutagens such as UV radiation or certain chemicals increase mutation rate.

problems.problem: (a) Name and describe two types of chromosomal mutation. (4 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Deletion is a chromosomal mutation in which a segment of a chromosome is lost or removed. This occurs when a chromosome breaks and the fragment is not reattached, often due to errors during crossing over or exposure to mutagens. The missing segment may be large, containing multiple genes. Deletions are usually harmful because essential genes are lost, and the effects depend on the size and location of the deletion. In humans, Cri-du-chat syndrome (cat's cry syndrome) is caused by a deletion on chromosome 5, resulting in intellectual disability, small head size, and a distinctive high-pitched cry. Deletions can also be homozygous lethal if essential genes are lost. Heterozygotes for a deletion may express recessive alleles that are normally masked (pseudodominance).

Duplication is a chromosomal mutation in which a segment of a chromosome is repeated one or more times. This occurs when a segment is copied (due to unequal crossing over or replication errors) and inserted adjacent to the original segment. Duplications can range from small (a few genes) to large (whole chromosome segments). While often less harmful than deletions, duplications can cause gene dosage effects (excess protein production) or provide raw material for evolution (since duplicated genes can acquire new functions without losing the original function). In humans, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A is caused by a duplication of the PMP22 gene on chromosome 17, leading to peripheral neuropathy. Duplications are also important in evolution: the globin gene family arose through repeated duplications of an ancestral gene.

Inversion is a chromosomal mutation in which a segment of a chromosome is reversed end-to-end. This occurs when a chromosome breaks in two places, and the intervening segment rotates 180° before rejoining. Inversions can be pericentric (including the centromere) or paracentric (not including the centromere). Individuals carrying inversions often have no phenotypic effects because the gene sequence is still present, but they can experience reduced fertility due to problems during meiosis (inversion loops leading to unbalanced gametes).

Translocation is a chromosomal mutation in which a segment of one chromosome breaks off and attaches to a non-homologous chromosome. Reciprocal translocations involve exchange of segments between two non-homologous chromosomes. Robertsonian translocations involve fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at the centromere. Translocations can cause cancer (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome in chronic myeloid leukemia, a reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22) and can lead to unbalanced gametes in carriers, resulting in miscarriages or children with congenital abnormalities.

problems.final_answer: (a) Deletion: loss of a chromosome segment (e.g., Cri-du-chat syndrome). Duplication: repetition of a chromosome segment (e.g., Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease). (Also accept inversion or translocation with description.)

problems.problem: Explain three sources of genetic variation in sexually reproducing organisms.

step_by_step.explanation: Crossing over occurs during Prophase I of meiosis. Homologous chromosomes pair up to form tetrads, and non-sister chromatids exchange segments of DNA at points called chiasmata. This process shuffles alleles between homologous chromosomes, creating new combinations of alleles on each chromatid. The result is that each gamete carries a unique combination of maternal and paternal genes that was not present in either parent. Without crossing over, the only variation would come from independent assortment of whole chromosomes. Crossing over dramatically increases genetic diversity. In humans, crossing over typically occurs 2-3 times per chromosome pair, resulting in over 2³ (8) different gamete types per chromosome pair from recombination alone, in addition to assortment.

Independent assortment occurs during Metaphase I of meiosis. When homologous chromosome pairs align at the metaphase plate, the orientation of each pair is random and independent of the orientation of other pairs. For each homologous pair, the maternal and paternal chromosomes can face either pole with equal probability. The number of possible arrangements is 2ⁿ, where n is the haploid number. In humans (n=23), this produces over 8 million (2²³ = 8,388,608) possible combinations of chromosomes in the resulting gametes, independent of crossing over. This random distribution of entire chromosomes ensures that each gamete receives a unique combination of maternal and paternal chromosomes, contributing enormous genetic variation among offspring.

Random fertilization refers to the fact that any male gamete (sperm) can fuse with any female gamete (egg) during sexual reproduction. In humans, a single male produces millions of sperm, each genetically unique due to crossing over and independent assortment. Similarly, each egg is genetically unique. The fusion of any one sperm with any one egg is a chance event, creating a zygote with a unique combination of alleles from both parents. With over 8 million possible gamete types from each parent (ignoring crossing over, which multiplies this number astronomically), the potential number of genetically distinct offspring is over 64 trillion (8 million × 8 million). This virtually ensures that each offspring (except identical twins) is genetically unique, providing the raw material for natural selection.

problems.final_answer: Three sources of genetic variation: (1) Crossing over—exchange of DNA between homologous chromosomes during prophase I. (2) Independent assortment—random orientation of homologous pairs during metaphase I. (3) Random fertilization—random fusion of any sperm with any egg.

problems.problem: (a) Define what is meant by 'selective breeding' (artificial selection). (2 marks) (b) Describe two advantages and two disadvantages of selective breeding in agriculture. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Selective breeding (also called artificial selection) is the intentional process by which humans breed plants or animals with desirable traits to produce offspring that inherit those traits. Over multiple generations, this process increases the frequency of desirable alleles in the population. Unlike natural selection, where environmental pressures determine reproductive success, selective breeding involves human choice as the selective pressure. Humans select individuals based on traits such as higher yield, disease resistance, faster growth rate, specific physical characteristics, or behavioral traits. The process has been used for thousands of years to domesticate crops and livestock, resulting in modern varieties that bear little resemblance to their wild ancestors (e.g., modern corn derived from teosinte, or domestic dogs from wolves).

Advantages: (1) Increased productivity: Selective breeding has dramatically increased agricultural yields. For example, modern dairy cows produce far more milk than their ancestors; wheat and rice varieties developed during the Green Revolution have higher yields, shorter stalks (preventing lodging), and improved disease resistance, contributing to food security for billions. (2) Improved quality: Selective breeding enhances desirable qualities such as taste, nutritional content, appearance, and shelf life. For instance, selectively bred crops have larger fruits, more uniform ripening, and better flavor; livestock can be bred for leaner meat or higher egg production. (3) Disease and pest resistance: Breeding for resistance reduces crop losses and the need for pesticides. For example, wheat varieties resistant to rust fungus, or cattle bred for resistance to bovine tuberculosis, reduce economic losses and improve food safety. (4) Adaptation to specific environments: Selective breeding can develop varieties suited to particular climates, soil conditions, or farming systems (e.g., drought-resistant crops, cold-tolerant livestock).

Disadvantages: (1) Reduced genetic diversity (genetic bottleneck): Intensive selection on a few desirable traits narrows the gene pool, making populations more vulnerable to diseases, pests, and environmental changes. For example, the Irish Potato Famine (1840s) was exacerbated because the potato crop was dominated by a few genetically uniform varieties (like the 'Lumper') that were all susceptible to late blight. Modern banana cultivation is threatened by Panama disease because the Cavendish variety (the dominant commercial banana) is genetically uniform. (2) Inbreeding depression: To fix desirable traits, breeders often mate closely related individuals (inbreeding), which increases homozygosity and can expose harmful recessive alleles. This can lead to reduced fertility, increased disease susceptibility, shorter lifespan, and other health problems. For example, some dog breeds suffer from hip dysplasia, breathing problems (brachycephalic breeds), or inherited disorders due to generations of inbreeding. (3) Unintended negative traits: Selection for one trait may inadvertently select for linked undesirable traits (pleiotropy). For instance, selection for rapid growth in broiler chickens has been associated with leg weakness and heart problems. (4) Ethical concerns: In animal breeding, selection for traits that maximize production (e.g., extremely rapid growth, high milk yield) can lead to animal welfare issues, such as skeletal problems, lameness, or reduced natural behaviors.

problems.final_answer: (a) Selective breeding is the intentional breeding of organisms with desirable traits to produce offspring with those traits. (b) Advantages: increased productivity (higher yields) and improved quality (better taste, disease resistance). Disadvantages: reduced genetic diversity (increased vulnerability to disease) and inbreeding depression (health problems from harmful recessive alleles).

problems.problem: (a) Describe the basic steps involved in genetic engineering to produce human insulin in bacteria. (4 marks) (b) Give two advantages of producing insulin this way compared to extracting it from animals. (2 marks)

step_by_step.step: Step 1: Isolation of the human insulin gene (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: The human insulin gene is isolated from human cells. Since the gene contains introns (non-coding sequences) that bacteria cannot remove, scientists typically use complementary DNA (cDNA) made from insulin mRNA. This is achieved by isolating insulin mRNA from human pancreatic beta cells, then using reverse transcriptase to synthesize cDNA that contains only the coding sequence (exons) for the insulin protein. Alternatively, the gene can be chemically synthesized. The gene is obtained with specific restriction enzyme recognition sites at each end to allow insertion into the plasmid vector.

step_by_step.step: Step 2: Preparation of the plasmid vector (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: A bacterial plasmid (small circular DNA molecule) is isolated from E. coli. The plasmid serves as a vector to carry the insulin gene into the bacteria. The plasmid is cut with the same restriction enzyme that was used to prepare the insulin gene. Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, producing complementary 'sticky ends' (overhanging single-stranded DNA) that can base-pair with the sticky ends of the insulin gene. The plasmid also contains a selectable marker gene (e.g., antibiotic resistance, such as ampicillin resistance) to identify bacteria that have taken up the plasmid.

The human insulin gene and the cut plasmid are mixed together with DNA ligase, an enzyme that catalyzes the formation of phosphodiester bonds between the sticky ends, joining the insulin gene into the plasmid. This creates a recombinant plasmid containing the human insulin gene. The recombinant plasmids are then introduced into bacterial cells (usually E. coli) through a process called transformation. Bacteria are treated with calcium chloride and heat shock to make their cell membranes permeable to DNA, allowing the plasmids to enter. Only a small proportion of bacteria take up the plasmid.

The transformed bacteria are grown on selective media containing an antibiotic (e.g., ampicillin). Only bacteria that have taken up the plasmid (which carries the antibiotic resistance gene) survive. These surviving colonies are screened to identify those containing the insulin gene (e.g., using colony hybridization or restriction analysis). The selected bacteria are then cultured in large fermenters. Under appropriate conditions (induction of the bacterial promoter controlling the insulin gene), the bacteria produce large quantities of human insulin protein. The insulin is then harvested, purified, and formulated for medical use. Modern production often uses E. coli or yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) as host organisms, with the insulin produced as a precursor that is later processed to the active form.

Advantages of recombinant human insulin compared to insulin extracted from animal sources (primarily pig and cattle pancreas): (1) No risk of immune reactions: Animal insulin differs from human insulin by 1-3 amino acids (porcine insulin differs by one amino acid; bovine insulin differs by three). Some patients developed allergic reactions or immune responses to animal insulin, leading to reduced efficacy over time. Recombinant human insulin is identical to human insulin, eliminating these immunological issues. (2) Unlimited supply and consistent quality: Animal insulin production was limited by the availability of animal pancreases (approximately 50 pigs were needed to produce enough insulin for one person for one year). Recombinant bacteria can produce unlimited quantities, ensuring a stable, reliable supply. The recombinant process also provides consistent purity and potency, whereas animal insulin could be contaminated with other pancreatic proteins. (3) Ethical advantages: Recombinant production eliminates reliance on slaughterhouse animals, addressing ethical concerns for some patients. (4) Scalability and cost: Large-scale fermentation is more cost-effective than extracting and purifying insulin from animal tissue, making insulin more affordable and accessible worldwide.

problems.final_answer: (a) Steps: (1) Isolate human insulin gene (or make cDNA from insulin mRNA); (2) Cut bacterial plasmid with same restriction enzyme; (3) Insert insulin gene into plasmid using DNA ligase; (4) Transform bacteria with recombinant plasmid; (5) Select transformed bacteria and culture to produce insulin. (b) Advantages: recombinant insulin is identical to human insulin (reduces allergic reactions) and can be produced in unlimited quantities (consistent supply).

problems.problem: (a) Explain the role of restriction enzymes in genetic engineering. (2 marks) (b) Explain why the same restriction enzyme is used to cut both the human gene and the bacterial plasmid. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Restriction enzymes (also called restriction endonucleases) are enzymes that cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, typically palindromic sequences 4-8 base pairs in length. They act as 'molecular scissors' that enable precise cutting of DNA at defined locations. In genetic engineering, restriction enzymes are used to: (1) Cut open the plasmid vector at a specific site to create a linearized plasmid capable of receiving an inserted gene. (2) Cut the donor DNA (e.g., human genomic DNA or cDNA) to isolate the gene of interest. The cuts can be staggered (creating sticky ends with single-stranded overhangs) or blunt (cutting straight across). Sticky ends are particularly useful because they can base-pair with complementary sticky ends from other DNA fragments, facilitating the joining of different DNA molecules. Different restriction enzymes recognize different sequences, giving scientists precise control over where DNA is cut.

step_by_step.step: Why the same restriction enzyme is used (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: The same restriction enzyme is used to cut both the human gene and the bacterial plasmid to ensure that the cut ends are complementary and can be joined together. Each restriction enzyme recognizes a specific DNA sequence and cuts at a defined position within or near that sequence. When the same enzyme cuts both DNA molecules, it produces identical sticky ends (or blunt ends) on both fragments. For example, if EcoRI (which recognizes GAATTC) is used, it cuts between G and A, producing overhangs of AATT on both the plasmid and the human gene. These complementary overhangs can base-pair (hydrogen bond) with each other, temporarily holding the human gene in place within the plasmid. DNA ligase then seals the sugar-phosphate backbone, creating a stable recombinant DNA molecule. If different restriction enzymes were used, the ends would not be complementary and the fragments could not join efficiently. This principle of using compatible cohesive ends is fundamental to constructing recombinant DNA molecules.

problems.final_answer: (a) Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, allowing precise isolation of genes and opening of plasmid vectors. (b) The same restriction enzyme is used to create complementary sticky ends on both the human gene and the plasmid, allowing them to base-pair and be joined by DNA ligase.

problems.problem: Cystic fibrosis is caused by a recessive allele. (a) State two symptoms of cystic fibrosis. (2 marks) (b) Explain why two parents who do not have cystic fibrosis can have a child with the disorder. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is caused by mutations in the CFTR gene (cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator), which encodes a chloride ion channel. Symptoms primarily affect the respiratory, digestive, and reproductive systems: (1) Respiratory symptoms: Thick, sticky mucus accumulates in the airways, leading to chronic coughing, recurrent lung infections (e.g., Pseudomonas aeruginosa), bronchiectasis (airway damage), and progressive respiratory failure. The mucus is difficult to clear, providing a breeding ground for bacteria. (2) Digestive symptoms: Thick mucus blocks the pancreatic ducts, preventing digestive enzymes from reaching the small intestine. This causes malabsorption of nutrients, leading to poor growth, weight loss (failure to thrive), steatorrhea (fatty, foul-smelling stools), and vitamin deficiencies (especially fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K). Many CF patients require pancreatic enzyme replacement with meals. (3) Other symptoms: Very salty sweat (due to defective chloride transport in sweat glands), meconium ileus in newborns (intestinal blockage), liver disease (bile duct blockage), and male infertility (absence of vas deferens).

step_by_step.step: Explanation of unaffected parents having affected child (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is caused by a recessive allele. Let F represent the normal (functional) CFTR allele and f represent the disease-causing (non-functional) allele. Two parents who do not have cystic fibrosis must each have at least one normal allele (F). If both parents are carriers (heterozygous, Ff), they do not have the disease because the normal allele is dominant and produces enough functional CFTR protein for normal chloride transport. However, when two carriers have children, each parent has a 50% chance of passing the recessive f allele to a child. The child inherits a recessive allele from both parents (ff) with a probability of 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4. This child will have cystic fibrosis because they lack any functional CFTR allele. This pattern—recessive disorders appearing in children of unaffected, carrier parents—explains why genetic conditions can 'skip' generations and why consanguineous (related) parents have higher risk of recessive disorders.
step_by_step.calculation:
Carrier parents: Ff × Ff
Offspring: 1 FF (unaffected), 2 Ff (carriers, unaffected), 1 ff (affected)
Probability of affected child: 1/4 (25%)

problems.final_answer: (a) Symptoms: persistent cough with thick mucus (respiratory) and poor weight gain/malabsorption (digestive). (b) Both parents can be carriers (heterozygous, Ff). They do not have the disorder because the normal allele is dominant, but each has a 50% chance of passing the recessive allele. If both pass it, the child is ff and has cystic fibrosis.

problems.problem: (a) Explain how the sickle cell allele causes abnormal red blood cells. (2 marks) (b) Explain why people who are heterozygous for sickle cell anaemia have some resistance to malaria. (2 marks) (c) State the type of selection that maintains the sickle cell allele in populations where malaria is common. (1 mark)

step_by_step.step: Mechanism of abnormal red blood cells (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: The sickle cell allele (often denoted as HbS) results from a point mutation in the β-globin gene: a single nucleotide change (GAG to GTG) causes the substitution of valine for glutamic acid at the sixth position of the β-globin chain. This change alters the properties of hemoglobin. Under low oxygen conditions (hypoxia), the abnormal hemoglobin S molecules polymerize (stick together) into long, rigid fibers. These fibers distort the red blood cell from its normal biconcave disc shape into a sickle (crescent) shape. The sickled cells are rigid and inflexible, causing them to become trapped in small blood vessels (capillaries), leading to vaso-occlusion, tissue ischemia (lack of oxygen), and pain crises. Sickled cells also have a reduced lifespan (10-20 days vs. 120 days for normal red blood cells), leading to chronic hemolytic anemia (shortage of red blood cells).

Individuals who are heterozygous for the sickle cell allele (HbA/HbS) have a selective advantage in regions where malaria (caused by Plasmodium falciparum) is endemic. They do not have sickle cell disease (which requires two copies of HbS), but they have a mixture of normal and sickled hemoglobin. The mechanism of protection involves: (1) Infected red blood cells with sickle hemoglobin tend to sickle more readily under the low-oxygen conditions within the parasite's life cycle, leading to premature destruction of parasitized cells before the parasite can complete its replication. (2) The sickling process impairs the ability of the malaria parasite to grow and multiply within the red blood cell. (3) There is reduced parasite adhesion to the endothelial lining of blood vessels in individuals with sickle cell trait. As a result, heterozygotes have significantly lower parasite loads and milder malaria symptoms compared to individuals with normal hemoglobin (HbA/HbA). This balancing selection maintains the harmful sickle cell allele in populations where malaria is common, despite the severe consequences for homozygotes.

The type of selection that maintains the sickle cell allele in malaria-endemic regions is balancing selection, specifically a form called heterozygote advantage (or overdominance). In heterozygote advantage, individuals who are heterozygous for a particular allele have a higher fitness than either homozygote. In this case: HbA/HbA homozygotes are susceptible to severe malaria; HbS/HbS homozygotes have sickle cell disease (severe anemia, pain crises, reduced life expectancy); HbA/HbS heterozygotes have resistance to malaria and do not have sickle cell disease. Therefore, the heterozygotes have the highest reproductive success, maintaining both alleles in the population. This is a classic example of balancing selection, where genetic variation is preserved because the heterozygote is fitter than either homozygote. The frequency of the HbS allele is highest (up to 20% in some populations) in regions of Africa, the Mediterranean, the Middle East, and India where malaria was historically prevalent.

problems.final_answer: (a) The sickle cell allele causes abnormal hemoglobin that polymerizes under low oxygen, distorting red blood cells into a sickle shape. (b) Heterozygotes (HbA/HbS) have some resistance because infected red blood cells sickle and are cleared, reducing parasite survival. (c) Balancing selection (heterozygote advantage).

problems.problem: Explain how environmental factors can influence gene expression without changing the DNA sequence.

step_by_step.explanation: Environmental factors can influence gene expression through epigenetic mechanisms—heritable changes in gene activity that do not involve alterations to the DNA sequence itself. Epigenetic modifications regulate whether genes are turned on or off, and to what extent they are expressed, without changing the underlying genetic code. These modifications can be influenced by factors such as diet, stress, temperature, exposure to toxins, and maternal behavior, and they can sometimes be passed to subsequent generations. Key epigenetic mechanisms include DNA methylation, histone modification, and non-coding RNA regulation.

DNA methylation involves the addition of a methyl group (-CH₃) to cytosine bases in DNA, typically at CpG islands (regions with high frequency of CpG dinucleotides) in promoter regions. Methylation of promoter regions generally represses gene expression by preventing transcription factors from binding to DNA or by recruiting proteins that promote a condensed chromatin structure. Environmental factors such as diet (e.g., folate and B vitamin availability affect methyl donor availability), stress, and exposure to pollutants can alter DNA methylation patterns. For example, studies have shown that maternal nutrition during pregnancy can affect methylation of genes involved in metabolism in offspring, influencing obesity risk later in life.

Histone modifications involve chemical changes to the histone proteins around which DNA is wrapped. Acetylation (addition of acetyl groups) of histones generally loosens the chromatin structure, making DNA more accessible to transcription factors and promoting gene expression. Deacetylation condenses chromatin and represses expression. Other modifications include methylation, phosphorylation, and ubiquitination. Environmental factors such as stress, hormones, and dietary components can influence histone-modifying enzymes. For example, chronic stress in rats has been shown to alter histone acetylation patterns in the brain, affecting genes involved in stress responses and behavior.

Non-coding RNAs (including microRNAs, long non-coding RNAs) can regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally by binding to mRNA and affecting its stability or translation. Environmental factors can alter the expression of these regulatory RNAs. For example, temperature changes in some plant species trigger expression of specific microRNAs that regulate genes involved in cold tolerance.

Concrete examples: (1) Temperature-dependent sex determination: In reptiles like alligators and turtles, the temperature at which eggs are incubated determines the sex of offspring by influencing expression of genes involved in gonad development. (2) Agouti mouse studies: Feeding pregnant mice a diet rich in methyl donors (e.g., folic acid, vitamin B12) alters DNA methylation of the agouti gene in offspring, affecting coat color and obesity risk. (3) Honeybee caste determination: Larvae fed royal jelly develop into queens, while those fed bee bread become workers; this difference is mediated by epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation) that alter gene expression patterns. (4) Human studies: Individuals exposed to famine during gestation (e.g., Dutch Hunger Winter of 1944-1945) showed altered DNA methylation patterns at genes related to growth and metabolism, with effects on health outcomes decades later.

problems.final_answer: Environmental factors influence gene expression through epigenetic mechanisms: DNA methylation (adding methyl groups to DNA, typically repressing genes) and histone modification (affecting chromatin structure), without changing the DNA sequence. Examples include diet affecting gene expression in offspring, temperature determining sex in reptiles, and nutrition influencing honeybee caste development.

problems.problem: Explain why the ABO blood group system is described as having multiple alleles, and explain how this differs from dihybrid inheritance.

step_by_step.explanation: The ABO blood group system is described as having multiple alleles because there are three different alleles (Iᴬ, Iᴮ, and Iᴼ) that can occupy the same gene locus (the ABO gene on chromosome 9). Multiple alleles means that more than two alternative forms of a gene exist within a population. However, any individual can carry only two of these alleles (one on each homologous chromosome), but the population as a whole has three possible variants. Multiple alleles often arise from mutations at the same locus over evolutionary time. The ABO alleles produce different antigens on red blood cells: Iᴬ produces A antigen, Iᴮ produces B antigen, and Iᴼ produces no antigen. The relationships among these alleles include codominance (Iᴬ and Iᴮ) and recessiveness (Iᴼ is recessive to both Iᴬ and Iᴮ).

Multiple alleles differ from dihybrid inheritance in several fundamental ways: (1) Number of genes involved: Multiple alleles involve variations at a single gene locus (one gene with multiple variants). Dihybrid inheritance involves two different genes (each with its own alleles) located on either the same or different chromosomes, controlling two distinct traits. (2) Alleles per individual: With multiple alleles, each individual carries only two alleles (one from each parent) for that single locus. In dihybrid inheritance, an individual carries two alleles for each of the two genes (four alleles total). (3) Phenotypic patterns: Multiple alleles produce a range of phenotypes based on the combination of the two alleles present (e.g., IᴬIᴬ and IᴬIᴼ both produce type A; IᴬIᴮ produces type AB). Dihybrid inheritance produces phenotypic ratios determined by the independent assortment of two genes, such as the classic 9:3:3:1 ratio in a cross between double heterozygotes (assuming independent assortment and complete dominance). (4) Complexity of inheritance: Multiple alleles require understanding of dominance hierarchies (often including codominance) among alleles at one locus. Dihybrid inheritance requires understanding the independent behavior of two separate loci. (5) Gamete formation: For a multiple-allele locus, a heterozygote (e.g., IᴬIᴮ) produces two types of gametes (Iᴬ and Iᴮ). For a dihybrid individual (e.g., AaBb), four types of gametes are produced (AB, Ab, aB, ab) assuming independent assortment.
step_by_step.calculation:
Multiple alleles: one locus, three+ alleles in population, individual carries two.
Dihybrid: two loci, each with two alleles (in typical examples), individual carries four alleles total.

problems.final_answer: ABO blood group has multiple alleles because three alleles (Iᴬ, Iᴮ, Iᴼ) exist at the same gene locus in the population. This differs from dihybrid inheritance, which involves two different genes (two loci) controlling two traits, each with two alleles (typically). Multiple alleles produce phenotypic patterns based on combinations at one locus; dihybrid inheritance produces 9:3:3:1 ratios from independent assortment of two loci.

problems.problem: A plant with purple flowers is known to be either homozygous dominant or heterozygous. Explain how a test cross can be used to determine its genotype, including expected results for each possibility.

step_by_step.explanation: A test cross is a method used to determine the genotype of an individual showing a dominant phenotype (but unknown whether homozygous dominant or heterozygous). The individual is crossed with a homozygous recessive individual for the same trait. By analyzing the phenotypic ratios in the offspring, the unknown genotype can be deduced. This works because the homozygous recessive parent contributes only recessive alleles to its offspring, so the phenotype of the offspring directly reveals the alleles contributed by the unknown parent. The test cross was one of Mendel's key experimental approaches to verify his laws.

In this case, the plant has purple flowers, with purple (P) dominant to white (p). The unknown plant is crossed with a white-flowered plant, which must be homozygous recessive (pp). The white-flowered plant produces only p gametes. The purple plant (unknown genotype) is either PP (homozygous dominant) or Pp (heterozygous). The two possibilities give different outcomes in the offspring.

step_by_step.step: Expected result if unknown plant is homozygous dominant (PP)
step_by_step.explanation: If the purple plant is PP, it produces only P gametes. The cross is PP × pp. All offspring will inherit P from the purple parent and p from the white parent, resulting in all Pp offspring. Since P is dominant, all offspring will have purple flowers. Therefore, if all offspring are purple, the unknown parent is homozygous dominant (PP).
step_by_step.calculation: PP (purple) × pp (white) → all Pp → 100% purple offspring

step_by_step.step: Expected result if unknown plant is heterozygous (Pp)
step_by_step.explanation: If the purple plant is Pp, it produces two types of gametes: P and p, in equal proportions (50% each). The cross is Pp × pp. The offspring will be: 50% Pp (purple) and 50% pp (white). This produces a 1:1 phenotypic ratio (1 purple : 1 white). Therefore, if approximately half the offspring are purple and half are white, the unknown parent is heterozygous (Pp).
step_by_step.calculation: Pp (purple) × pp (white) → 1/2 Pp (purple), 1/2 pp (white) → 1:1 phenotypic ratio

problems.final_answer: Perform a test cross by crossing the purple-flowered plant with a white-flowered (homozygous recessive) plant. If all offspring are purple, the unknown plant is homozygous dominant (PP). If the offspring show a 1:1 ratio of purple to white, the unknown plant is heterozygous (Pp).

problems.problem: In a population, 16% of individuals have a recessive genetic condition. (a) Calculate the frequency of the recessive allele. Show your working. (2 marks) (b) Calculate the frequency of the dominant allele. (1 mark) (c) Calculate the percentage of heterozygous individuals in the population. Show your working. (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: The Hardy-Weinberg principle states that for a gene with two alleles (dominant A and recessive a) in a population at equilibrium, the frequencies follow: p + q = 1, where p = frequency of dominant allele, q = frequency of recessive allele. The genotype frequencies are: p² for AA (homozygous dominant), 2pq for Aa (heterozygous), and q² for aa (homozygous recessive). In this problem, the recessive genetic condition (aa) affects 16% of individuals, so q² = 0.16.

The frequency of the recessive allele (q) is the square root of the frequency of homozygous recessive individuals (q²). q = √q² = √0.16 = 0.4. Therefore, the recessive allele frequency is 0.4 (or 40%).
step_by_step.calculation:
q² = 0.16
q = √0.16 = 0.4
Recessive allele frequency = 0.4 (40%)

step_by_step.explanation: Since p + q = 1, the frequency of the dominant allele (p) is 1 - q = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6. Therefore, the dominant allele frequency is 0.6 (or 60%).
step_by_step.calculation:
p = 1 - q = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
Dominant allele frequency = 0.6 (60%)

step_by_step.explanation: The frequency of heterozygous individuals (Aa) is given by 2pq. Using p = 0.6 and q = 0.4: 2pq = 2 × 0.6 × 0.4 = 2 × 0.24 = 0.48. Therefore, 48% of the population are heterozygous carriers (assuming Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium conditions hold).
step_by_step.calculation:
2pq = 2 × 0.6 × 0.4 = 2 × 0.24 = 0.48
Heterozygous frequency = 48%

problems.final_answer: (a) Recessive allele frequency (q) = √0.16 = 0.4 (40%). (b) Dominant allele frequency (p) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 (60%). (c) Heterozygous frequency = 2pq = 2 × 0.6 × 0.4 = 0.48 (48%).

problems.problem: A rare genetic disorder is caused by a recessive allele. A couple planning to have children both have a sibling with the disorder, but neither parent of the couple is affected. (a) State the probability that the male partner is a carrier. Explain your reasoning. (3 marks) (b) Calculate the probability that their first child will have the disorder. Show your working. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: The disorder is recessive. Let A represent the normal allele and a represent the disease allele. The affected sibling has the genotype aa. Both parents of the affected sibling (the grandparents of the couple's potential children) are not affected but have an affected child. Therefore, both grandparents must be carriers (heterozygous, Aa), because they each passed a recessive allele to the affected child. The male partner (the focus of part a) is the sibling of the affected individual. The male partner's parents are Aa × Aa. The male partner is not affected (so he is not aa). He could be either AA or Aa. Using Mendelian ratios from Aa × Aa, the possible unaffected offspring genotypes are: 1 AA : 2 Aa. Since he is known to be unaffected, the probability he is a carrier (Aa) is 2/3 (because among unaffected offspring, two out of three are carriers).
step_by_step.calculation:
Grandparents: Aa × Aa
Unaffected offspring genotypes: 1 AA : 2 Aa
P(carrier | unaffected) = 2/3

step_by_step.step: Probability both parents are carriers (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: The same reasoning applies to the female partner: she also has an affected sibling, and her parents are also unaffected carriers (Aa × Aa). Therefore, the probability she is a carrier is also 2/3 (assuming no additional family history that changes this probability). The couple's child will have the disorder (aa) only if both parents are carriers AND both pass the recessive allele. If either parent is not a carrier (AA), the child cannot be affected (though could be a carrier). The probability that both parents are carriers is (2/3) × (2/3) = 4/9.

step_by_step.step: Probability child is affected given both are carriers (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: If both parents are carriers (Aa × Aa), the probability that their child is affected (aa) is 1/4. This is the standard Mendelian ratio for a recessive disorder from two heterozygotes.

The overall probability that the child will have the disorder is the probability that both parents are carriers multiplied by the probability that the child is affected given both are carriers. This is (4/9) × (1/4) = 4/36 = 1/9. There is also a small chance that one parent is a carrier and the other is not, but that cannot produce an affected child. Additionally, the probability that either parent is AA is considered in the calculation of the joint carrier probability. The final probability is 1/9.
step_by_step.calculation:
P(both carriers) = (2/3) × (2/3) = 4/9
P(affected | both carriers) = 1/4
P(affected) = (4/9) × (1/4) = 4/36 = 1/9

problems.final_answer: (a) The male partner has a 2/3 chance of being a carrier. His unaffected parents must both be carriers (since they had an affected child), and among unaffected offspring, 2/3 are carriers. (b) Probability child has disorder = (2/3 × 2/3) × (1/4) = 4/9 × 1/4 = 1/9.
